title: Theater Program Review AY 2021-2022 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Review- Theatre Arts AY 2021-22
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
While the Covid-19 pandemic upended many of the theater program’s plans for the academic
year we able to mitigate some of its effects despite the wide spread disruption it caused. As
mentioned in previous program reviews, the theater staff and faculty have a dynamic and
diverse skill set allowing us to be very adaptive to challenges, even world-wide events.
The Theater program remains committed to 3 key areas; recruitment, retention, and award
completion, which keeps us in alignment with the College of the Siskiyou’s institutional goals.
Recruitment
Even though, over the years, we have created strong relationships with local high schools and
K-12 regional arts programs which has resulted in a steady increase in our student head counts,
our recruitment efforts have been limited due to Covid-19 restrictions.
As high school performing arts teachers struggle to keep their programs alive while they adhere
to necessary social distancing protocols we have attempted to reach out and offer support by
doing the following;
Emailed high school arts educators offering support
Reached out to high students with audition invitations for both Fall 2020 and Spring
2021 Zoom productions
Retention and Degree Completion
Over the years, we have been able to continue to have good retention numbers largely due to
producing diverse and engaging productions which keep students attracted to the program.
Also our established transfer relationship with SOU as well as our participation in the Kennedy
Center American College Theater Festival, helps to create a viable and visible road to degree
completion.
We have offered “Zoom” productions in both the Fall 2020 and Spring 2021 semesters
in and attempt to keep students engaged.
We have moved courses required for theater degrees and certificates to either online or
hybrid formats in an effort to provide a means towards degree completion.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
No progress due to institutional lack of commitment.

-- 1 of 3 --

title: Theater Program Review AY 2021-2022 (Part 2)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Review- Theatre Arts AY 2021-22
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
Moving classes to online and hybrid formats
The Covid-19 pandemic necessitated moving classes to either hybrid or online format which,
lucky for the theater program, was one of our long-term goals. In fact, this year we were able to
teach all of our classes in this manner which helped to mitigate some of the student attrition
due to the disruption of face to face instruction.
We were also able to successfully offer all “Zoom” productions in both semesters;
Fall 2020 “Shakespeare’s Greatest Hits”
Spring 2021 “ Night Night Roger Roger”
Hiring of Adjunct Theater Instructor
This year we were fortunate to be able to hire an Adjunct Theater Instructor, Kitty Keim, who
was able to adapt to teaching both acting classes and directing each of our productions via
Zoom.
We have been proactive in taking advantage of opportunities. We have several talented
teachers able to teach at the non-credit level so we have begun to create non-credit classes and
certificates which has led to a sharp increase in K-12 students and non-traditional students
participating with our program, leading to increases in FTEs.
Increase our Non-Credit course offerings
To better serve both our degree seeking students and community members seeking enrichment
the Theater program is exploring adding a series of non-credit classes to either; accompany
credit courses so that community members can experience a more rigorous educational
environment, or augment already existing credit courses to better prepare degree seeking
students.
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications.
Restore Theater Performance Instructor
While the single full-time faculty member has volunteered to teach in overload since 2011,
largely to insure that students achieve Theater degrees and certificates, this has led to the delay
of the creation of an AA in Technical Theater, and other important curricular development. The
full-time faculty member must serve as stage director, technical director, and set and lighting

-- 2 of 3 --

College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Review- Theatre Arts AY 2021-22
designer for all productions pertaining to traditional tranfer level courses. The current faculty
member does possess all of the required skills to perform all of these roles but it’s not possible
to perform them all at the same time. Over the past several years an attempt has been made to
hire adjunct instructors to help teach the required courses for the various theater degreees but
this has proven to be ineffective. The region simply does not have a significant pool of qualified
instructors.
Based on this evidence it is strongly recommended that the College of the Siskiyous hire
another full-time theater faculty to teach classes in performance and share the management
theater program. Please see CQIP 1.

-- 3 of 3 --
